topics.headline: Education Recovery Scorecard — recovery has stalled, and the gap is now structural
topics.why_matters: This is the highest-evidence-strength item in the window. Peer-reviewed methodology, district-level granularity, and a clear finding: the recovery curve has flattened, and ESSER's expiration removed the funding that was driving what little progress existed. That is *exactly* the gap A+'s high-impact tutoring model is ESSA-evidence-rated to close. It is also the cleanest "the data says what we've been saying" moment A+ has had since the original Reardon work landed.
topics.angle: Lead with the scorecard data, then pivot to: the schools closing the gap are the ones that redirected ESSER-era intervention into permanent Title I/III line items before the cliff hit. Frame A+ as the implementation partner for charters that made that pivot.
topics.roman_take: "The recovery didn't stall because kids can't learn. It stalled because we defunded the only thing that was working." — argue that the scorecard is a referendum on whether districts treated tutoring as a COVID expense or as the permanent Tier 2/3 layer it always should have been.
topics.danielle_take: Walk through what it looks like operationally to convert ESSER-era tutoring spend into a sustainable Title I + Title III braided budget — the exact conversation she's having with partner schools right now (reference Ocean Grove / South Sutter / Visions implementation pattern without naming).

topics.headline: LCAP public hearing season — what charter boards are (and aren't) funding for Tier 2/3 intervention in FY26-27
topics.why_matters: This is the single window where intervention spend gets locked in for the next fiscal year. A+ has earned the right to comment because A+ is *in* several of these LCAPs already as a named provider or implied service. Any partner whose LCAP draft under-funds Tier 2/3 supports relative to their CAASPP/ELPAC gap is a live conversation Danielle should already be having — and a LinkedIn post that names the pattern (without naming the school) gives her the on-ramp.
topics.angle: "It's LCAP hearing season. Here are the three line items charter directors should be able to defend on intervention — and the one question every board member should be asking before they vote."
topics.roman_take: The honest conviction post — most LCAPs treat intervention as a compliance narrative, not a budget commitment, and that's why kids stay LTEL for seven years.
topics.danielle_take: The practitioner walk-through — what a defensible Tier 2/3 line item actually looks like in an LCAP, with the four data points she'd want to see before approving one.

topics.headline: AI tutoring platforms are scaling into K-12 intervention contracts without ESSA Tier 1 or 2 evidence
topics.why_matters: Charter directors on the HOT 13 list and across the Tier A partner base are being pitched AI tutoring as a low-cost intervention substitute right now. The pitch is compelling on price; it collapses on evidence and on EL/IEP service delivery. A+ has the only authentic counter-position in our partner conversations: human-led, evidence-aligned, billable to Title I/III, defensible in CARS. This is the differentiation conversation of the next 12 months, and almost nobody in the intervention space is willing to say it cleanly because they all have AI roadmaps.
topics.angle: Frame the ESSA evidence ladder. Show where AI tutoring sits today (no Tier 1/2 designations for intervention) versus where high-impact tutoring sits (multiple Tier 1 studies). End on the question charter directors should ask any vendor: "Show me your Tier."
topics.roman_take: The conviction take — "An intervention without evidence isn't an intervention, it's a subscription." Push on the moral hazard of selling unproven tools to the kids with the least margin for error.
topics.danielle_take: The implementation take — what an ELL coordinator actually has to document to defend an AI-only intervention in a CARS audit or an IEP team meeting, and why that paper trail doesn't exist yet for the AI products being pitched.
